The right think to do would be to cancel any scheduled executions once a new connection is established. As it is I don't see any reference return value being produced to support cancelation. The next alternative is to store a flag within the bot which marks whether a connection check is scheduled and pending. If upon the next disconnect a check is pending there is no need to schedule another. This should break the pitfall sequence which miss currently possible.

I think it would help in creating more efficient fuzzed messages knowing that only certain values are used at any particular bit position.

So for example if i were to fuzz position 3&4 of a particular transmission rather then sending every possible hex value it would be nice to know based on the statistics of the recorded sample that the only values used are say A,B,1,2,3

Hope that makes sense.

@aghayoor @hjmjohnson What is your opinion on the following? I propose to turn your contributions on handling dwi files into a proper submodule of nrrd ("nrrd.dwi"). This way, users of the library can for example refer to the function nrrd.dwi.ReadNAMICDWIFromNrrd.

After some reading and experimentation I believe this can be done as follows:
Rename nrrd_dwi.py to dwi.py 
Edit setup.py: remove the line with py_modules= and add the following two lines instead:
packages=['nrrd'],
      package_dir={'nrrd': ''},
Add a __init__.py with the following content:
# import all of nrrd
from nrrd import *
# import sub-module for handling nrrd files with dwi content
import dwi
What do you think?

As for the tests, I'll for now take your word on that it all works as I cannot test the code myself. It would be nice if there eventually would be a couple of small (synthetic?) test files so that we can automatically test also that part of the library.

—

1. https://github.com/Kyria/EsiPy/blob/master/esipy/security.py#L56 change this line to use [ESI verify](https://esi.tech.ccp.is/verify/) instead of SSO verify (the most important change of this issue actually)
2. https://github.com/Kyria/EsiPy/blob/master/esipy/security.py#L64 change this to a default (tranquility) URL, unless an "app" object (like the current behavior) or a "base sso url" (login.eveonline.com) is provided, to be able to create security object before having to make an "app" 

The 2. is actually just a comfort change and won't change the current behavior, but just add some parameter to `EsiSecurity.__init__()`
The tests finally passed! There is no report to comparate against because we just introduced it :) So @Turbo87, ready for review!

Well, more than compressing (as images are usually already compressed and compressing already compressed data might only make it bigger by adding compression header and metadata twice) you should probably tune the format, quality, size of the image. That can also be done using a DEPOT filter or custom attachment that uses Pillow to re-encode the image at lower quality or with another format.

If you are using DepotMiddleware to serve files from S3 it won't actually serve them, it will just perform and HEAD request to check that they exist and will then redirect the user to S3. That's the behaviour for any storage backend that provides public urls like S3.

If you are using the database layer it won't even perform the HEAD request as it will directly return the public url as the .`url` property of the file.
One way to do this is to use `$ENVIRON`, loaded with info from a fixture.

I've been doing a lot of thinking about this whole subject over the last few days and I'm afraid that {allow|defer}_cancellation is going on the chopping block.   The semantics are too complicated and the current implementation is logically unsound in the presence of nesting.   I don't know if adding tokens saves it or not, but I do know that "if the implementation is hard to explain, it's a bad idea" is part of the Zen of Python ;-).   I also know that my head is starting to explode. 

I think a better solution to this whole problem is going to be cancellation polling.  As an option, it's easy to make it so that tasks NEVER receive a CancelledError exception.   Instead, a flag can be set that must be checked via explicit polling::

    async def supervisor(self):
            myself = await current_task()
            while self._tasks and not self._shutting_down:
                    whatever
                    ...
                    if myself.cancelled:            # Have I been cancelled? 
                          await self._start_shutdown()

When launching the supervisor, you'd supply some kind of option to `spawn()` to not deliver a CancelledError exception:  

      task = await spawn(self.supervisor(), allow_cancellation=False)

Timeouts, although related to cancellation, are a different kind of animal.  Timeouts can be handled and ignored.  The semantics of nesting them is more well-defined.  I'm not inclined to change how they currently work.    If the above supervisor is concerned about getting a timeout from outside, then don't apply a timeout to it. Or wrap it:

      task = await spawn(self.supervisor(), allow_cancellation=False)
      try:
             await task.join()
      except TaskTimeout:
             await task.cancel()

We can't possibly cover 100% of the cases. We will end up with instances of providers that are very similar to each other except for that one difference that matters to that one person.

Currently, you can create a custom provider (http://fake-factory.readthedocs.org/en/stable/index.html#how-to-create-a-provider). You could include it in your project or, if you want to reuse it across projects, make it its own python package and link to it.

That said, I'm interested in finding a longer term solution to this issue.

I'm willing to maintain a list of community-supported providers in the docs, but I'm -0 on putting them under a `faker` umbrella org, because that puts the burdain of those packages (taking care of code, community, governance, etc) to the maintainers of `faker` (ie: me and @joke2k).

Would be really cool if faker had its own 'packages store' where you can discover and download providers from, but that involves server expenses we don't have the resources for.

Without knowing more about "not nearly as good", the only suggestion that comes to mind is [arithmetic underflow](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arithmetic_underflow), which results in a loss of precision. Underflow is especially bad when multiplying decimals with large non-whole-number components, which is common for probabilities.

By using log-scale values, we can use addition instead of multiplication, which is less prone to underflow errors, while being able to get back the normal scale value at the end. No matter what, we're losing precision due to using IEEE floating point value representation, but using log scale values keeps this to a minimum.

The issue here is that `db.update` needs an iterable of features, but the generator is creating an iterable of lists-of-features. Easiest way around that is to go one loop deeper in the generator, making sure it yields features and not lists of features. Changing the generator to the following did the trick:

```python
import gffutils
import os

genomeGFF = 'test.gff'
genomeGFFdb = 'test.db'

db = gffutils.create_db(genomeGFF, dbfn=genomeGFFdb, force=True, merge_strategy="merge")

def generatorModifyFeatures(featureFamilies):
    for featureFamily in featureFamilies:
        for i in featureFamily:
            print(i)
            yield i

featureFamilies = list(db.iter_by_parent_childs())
db.update(generatorModifyFeatures(featureFamilies), merge_strategy="replace")
```

Files manipulated in the bucket outside of a yas3fs cluster will not "notify" yas3fs of any new items in S3. You will either have to

I just tried it on Ubuntu and don't get those linker errors.  Cygwin can be a fickle beast sometimes.  Don't worry about them for the moment.  The bigger issue is the double building since that doubles the build time of existing dlib projects.  But on the whole this PR is getting pretty good :)

Anyway, here is what I think you can do to fix the double building problem.  You should be able to set a variable in the dlib/cmake file that is something like `set(you_are_being_invoked_from_another_project_so_don't_make_the_shared_library 1)`.  You get the idea, but pick a more sensible variable name :).  Then make the install/shared lib building in dlib/CMakeLists.txt conditional on that variable.

Also, see if you can tell cmake to install not just the shared library but also the archive since we are building both and it's nice to include both when installed anyway.  I'm sure other users will ask for it as well.

As for renaming ENABLE_ASSERTS, yeah, you are right.  But it's too late to rename it now as that would break backwards compatibility.  If it were causing trouble then maybe, but no one has ever encountered problems with it so it's best to leave it alone.

Also, it's a good idea to include(release_build_by_default) (or do something similar) so that cmake will compile dlib with optimizations enabled.  Right now when you do the `cmake dlib; make install` it compiles it without any optimizations.

! main.f90
program main

  implicit none

  integer, parameter :: dp = kind(0.d0)

  real(dp) :: left(4, 50)
  real(dp) :: right(50, 2)
  real(dp) :: product_(4, 2)
  integer:: i, j

  do i = 1, 4
     do j = 1, 50
        left(i, j) = i + j
        if (i < 3) then
           right(j, i) = i * i - j
        end if
     end do

end program main

It had to be sharp edge between faces enclosing an acute angle - so a cube wouldn’t do the trick.
Therefore I constructed a small tetrahedron with a sharp edge and some points to demonstrate it in a test scenario.

However, on the workstation where I encountered the issue, the trimesh and scipy versions are a bit outdated. On another machine where I have recent versions installed I could not reproduce the "false sign" behavior. It had something to do with the order of faces in the trimesh.faces array.

Nevertheless, I edited my fix so that the returned face-index of the closest face is at least the one that is actually facing the querying point.

> To schedule a callback from a different thread, the `AbstractEventLoop.call_soon_threadsafe()` method should be used.

You can not use `asyncio.get_event_loop` to get loop object here.

Hmm, there might be a couple problems with that:

 - you can never use SETCONF safely without doing GETCONF first (for example if you "SETCONF SocksPort=1234" without any of the other ones configured before, you'll clobber any other SOCKS ports.
 - the `Tor` controller object does use SETCONF for SOCKSPorts; this is for the APIs that allow you to specify a socks-port (e.g. the .socks_endpoint() factory-method).
 - ...so "just use SETCONF" isn't very good, because it's "pretty low level" and easy to get wrong

So perhaps we really do want 2 variants of `TorConfig`: one that tries to do "minimal" GETCONF queries and one that doesn't.

But perhaps a different alternative is to just `try/except` around the GETCONF we have and just set it to some sentinel value (that causes an exception later if you try and change the value). This would let it work slightly better against a filtering proxy, but also would make it harder to know if (or which) GETCONF calls you'd have to whitelist for your application...

There are actually two sides of this story:
1) whether to have tests in the tar.gz (this is controlled by MANIFEST.in)
2) whether to install them (this is controlled by setup.py)

Currently psutil does both. I prefer to include tests because: 

1) each release is "set in stone" and tests are changing over time, so one should have the possibility to download an old psutil version, unpack it and run tests
2) they indirectly serve as a documentation tool; one can grep for a psutil API and find both the definition of the API and how it is used by tests

I'm pretty sure most open source projects include tests in the tar.gz and I see no reason not to. The same goes for doc, for instance. Doc is part of the release/distribution and as such is included in the tarball.
The reason I prefer to install them is:

3) I find it useful that one has the possibility to just `python -c "import psutil.tests; psutil.tests.run()"` and verify their installation fully works.

Using 0.3.19, importing the exception both via root import and the netlink package now work.

So, possible exceptions (see the test code above, https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/commit/86e3f0cd5a9ffff8cfcdfdf73d438923e05db62d):
* `socket.error == 90` — either NLA or the whole message exceed netlink limits (they're slightly lower then the encoding format)
* `struct.error` — either NLA or the whole message exceed the encoding format limits (max length: uint16 for NLA and uint32 for the whole message)

`SO_SNDBUF` matters only if the kernel code works slower than the userspace (not the case with Python), and it doesn't affect one message, whatever big may it be.

If there is no error, that means that the data successfully passed the netlink API on the kernel side and was transmitted to the kernel module code. There should be the debugging directed.

Unfortunately, the previous «dynamic» modules loading was a kind of nasty hack, that made some hidden magic, and personally I'm happy to get rid of it (just try to take alook at the previous «dynamic» version of the root module — https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/blob/0.4.17/pyroute2/__init__.py)

But I'm sorry I broke the API for the plugins (actually, the dark side of the implicit magic — you never know, when it gets broken). So yes, I'm to document the explicit way.

There shouldn't be any need to use colorama in IPython notebooks (at least, not for conversion - you're still free to use the styling API). The IPython frontend contains its own machinery to translate ANSI escapes into HTML sequences (and it won't do anything with Windows console API calls). So any issues you're seeing are in IPython (or rather, Jupyter, which is a new name for the parts of the project that aren't specific to Python - see the [jupyter/notebook](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook)) repo.

It looks like the distinction between dim and bright has gone missing, but the notebook machinery obviously doesn't know about colorama's `autoreset`, so it's expected that that has no effect.

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import PIL.Image

label_file = 'label.png'
lbl = np.asarray(PIL.Image.open(label_file))
print(np.unique(lbl))
assert lbl.dtype == np.int32

for label_value in np.unique(lbl):
    mask = lbl == label_value
    plt.imshow(mask, gray=True)
    plt.show()
```

Firstly, for perfloop, don't use the `diff_to_previous` framebuffer - you need to choose `full_frame` to get a realistic measure of worst-case FPS. The diff-to-previous does exactly what it says - it chooses the bit of the frame that was different to last time, and just renders that - and because the perfloop doesn't change, it doesn't render anything.

Thanks for the replies! I understand where you're coming from. If you want to leave things the way they are then I respect that. I'd like to explain my thinking, though.

I don't know why anybody—superuser or not—would need to add or edit a drf-tracking record. The purpose of drf-tracking, as I understand it, is to log real requests made to the site's API. If somebody adds or edits a record from the admin then drf-tracking will have inaccurate information since the record won't be a representation of a real request.

I think it makes sense to have the most likely configuration be the default configuration and if somebody wants to override it then they can. So, in my opinion, drf-tracking's default configuration should be that records cannot be added or edited from the admin. If somebody really wants to be able to add or edit drf-tracking records from the admin (and, again, I don't know why they would) then they can enable that themselves. That's just my opinion, though.

Sorry, I know that this took some effort but I really don't like this approach. Caching is a trade off between speed and memory and I feel it shouldn't be forced onto the user. The user can best gauge if it really is necessary and then it's not too hard to do it on a per case basis. Additionally it makes the code needlessly complex - I'm for keeping it as simple as possible. Do you think there are realistic use cases where caching provides a meaningful improvement?

@jrkerns, yes, there are a lot of complications to figure out.  However, I think I may have found a potential way through all of it.  See the [iod branch](https://github.com/darcymason/pydicom/commit/7ab8823039b0b30549e5e42c66eee4cd73cd9754) for a proof of principle start on this. The idea is that a "Module" defines a list of tags that it "owns".  The modules register themselves with Dataset -- If any of the tags is called in the dataset, it is hooked into the module class to get or set them.  This can preserve backwards compatibilty, because user code can simply turn off the module hooks to run old code.  Still lots to work out here in terms of pixels.  For example I'm fairly sure a numpy array of pixels by itself does not uniquely determine number of planes and colour planes vs gray scale etc. So we might be back to a set_image() type function as Alex has mentioned.

@cancan101, you make a good point about transfer syntax.  However, I read that with the emphasis on the word "transfer", meaning the syntax can be whatever we want until communicating it to someone else.  So it should be possible to leave the pixel data in whatever representation we want internally, until the point where it is written somewhere else.  I would argue that after reading a file, regardless of transfer syntax, if someone tried to access the pixels they would want them decompressed or else they cannot be sensibly interpreted anyway.  So the ImagePixel module would keep track of the original syntax, convert when pixels requested, and when writing, reconvert if pixels have been changed or if the transfer syntax was changed.  It would require some internal flags, but i think it wouldn't be too bad.

Quoting the docs at http://happybase.readthedocs.org/en/latest/user.html#manipulating-data:

> HBase does not have any notion of data types; all row keys, column names and column values are simply treated as raw byte strings. By design, HappyBase does not do any automatic string conversion. This means that data must be converted to byte strings in your application before you pass it to HappyBase, for instance by calling str() or by employing more advanced string serialisation techniques like struct.pack().

Something like `struct.pack('>Q', 12345)` allows you to encode 64-bit unsigned integers. Depending on your application needs, smaller values may suffice.

But it looks like you're also using `.counter_inc()`, in which case you should (again) have read in the documentation at http://happybase.readthedocs.org/en/latest/user.html#using-atomic-counters that a `.counter_set()` method can be used, e.g.

```python
table.counter_set('row-key', 'cf1:counter', 12345)
```

So if you're using the counters API, stick to the functions that deal with those, such as `.counter_inc()`, `.counter_get()`, and `.counter_set()`.

I'm going to propose the following stylistic guidelines while contributing type annotations.

- Follow PEP 8.
- Use `mimesis.typing` for defining Mimesis-only type variables, such as `TimeStamp`, `JSON`, etc. It's actually rather annoying to have to keep typing `types.Any`, when we should be importing them directly from Python's `typing` module at the top of the file, i.e. `from typing import Any`.
- For type checking I'm using the following command (are there any additional restrictions you'd like to impose?): `mypy --strict --strict-optional --check-untyped-defs`

Another thing I'm seeing over and over again is the use of spaces before default arguments, e.g. the following, which can be found in `helpers.py`:

> def randints(self, amount: int = 3, a: int = 1, b: int = 100) -> list:

This should instead be, per PEP 8 guidelines,

> def randints(self, amount: int =3, a: int =1, b: int =100) -> list:

The commit runs against the `development` branch rather than the usual pull request approach which is doing that against the `master` branch.

`backtrader` tries to follow this development guidelines:

  - `master` is *always guaranteed* to work and in sync with the releases

    Sometimes you may find 1 or 2 extra commits in `master` which usually just add/correct documentation or add a new sample.

    Under *guaranteed to work* one has to make a small leap of faith because not every corner and use case can be covered and sometimes, something may finally show up as broken.

  - `development` has no guarantees

    It will in most cases work and work as expected, but the current commit may simply be the push of a working state which may be completely broken.

In this case things were working and working as expected, but a test case for the new indicator `LaguerreRSI` was prepared but not finished and the CI complained about the test suite not being able to complete.

In any case the *not being subscribed* distracted me and failed to see that your `518` is actually caused by this:

  - `Request Contract Data Sending Error - 'int' object has no attribute 'encode'`

This has obviously nothing to do with being subscribed or not. The easiest educated guess is:

  - Are you using Python 3.x? (because of the `encode` hint in the error message)

```
  self.longshortpos=1

This is an int. If you want to declare a line you have to declare them (in the `Strategy` or in an `Indicator`) See here

  - http://backtrader.readthedocs.io/en/latest/concepts.html#lines-declaration

```python
class SimpleMovingAverage(Indicator):
    lines = ('sma',)
```

`if` and `and`can not be overriden due to the nature of Python. See here:

  - http://backtrader.readthedocs.io/en/latest/concepts.html#some-non-overriden-operators-functions

You have to use `backtrader.If` and `backtrader.And`

During the `__init__` phase the operations do not return values, they return objects which will later in the background be evaluated (lazy evaluation). The user can then in each `next` invocation access the values.

But in this case this `if` logic has to be used in `next`. Accessing `position` (which is not a line but the actual position) only makes sense during `next` when `position` may have actually changed.

> Is this an excel issue

Yes. Excel sometimes adjust the height of the row automatically but this cannot be controlled from the file format. Therefore, you shouldn't rely on this and you should explicitly set the height of the row.

This is particularly important if the worksheet has images or chart or comments that cross those rows since the module requires the row height to calculate the position/size of these objects.


> Setting the row height manually is not very precise as it's very difficult to accurately calculate how many rows a string needs...

That is unfortunately true. You will need to come up with some workaround calculation based on the number of newlines in the string or the string length or something similar.

There isn't anything that the module can do to make this happen automatically.

Regards,

John

Hi,

You can post code directly in Github like this:

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbookName = "BUG.xlsx"
print "WorkbookName: ", workbookName
workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook(workbookName)

worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet("bug")

worksheet.write('A1', 'Sample Number'); worksheet.write('B1', 50)
worksheet.write('A2', 'STD');           worksheet.write('B2', 2);

## The error is given in the two formulas intrududed in B3 and B4.
# I think suspect that the error is given by the comma.

worksheet.write('A3', 'CONF. NORM');    
worksheet.write('B3', "=CONFIDENCE.NORM(0,05;B2;B1)");
worksheet.write('A4', 'CONF. Manaul');  
worksheet.write('B4', "=1,36*B2/B5*^,2");

workbook.close()
```

There are a number of issues here. 

* The main one is that formulas in XlsxWriter have to be written in US Excel style. That includes using a `,` instead of `;` as a separator. That is explained in the [write_formula() docs](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/worksheet.html#worksheet-write-formula).

* Also you have to use `.` for decimals like you would in Python: `1,36` -> `1.36`.

* Also, the formulas has to be valid and I don't think `B2/B5*^.2` is. That should probably be `B2/B5^.2`.

* Finally, `CONFIDENCE.NORM` is an Excel "future" function that was added in Excel 2010/2013 so it has be written as `_xlfn.CONFIDENCE.NORM()`. This is also explained in the [write_formula() docs](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/worksheet.html#worksheet-write-formula).

Making these changes, the output file will open correctly:

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbookName = "NO_BUGS.xlsx"
print "WorkbookName: ", workbookName

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook(workbookName)
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.set_column('A:A', 16)

worksheet.write('A1', 'Sample Number');
worksheet.write('B1', 50)
worksheet.write('A2', 'STD');
worksheet.write('B2', 2);

worksheet.write('A3', 'CONF. NORM');
worksheet.write('B3', "=_xlfn.CONFIDENCE.NORM(0.05,B2,B1)");
worksheet.write('A4', 'CONF. Manaul');
worksheet.write('B4', "=1.36*B2/B5^.2");
worksheet.write('B5', 1);

workbook.close()

```

Regards,

John.

Hi everyone. Here is a solution that may work for you. All you need to do is create a file containing the following logic adapter and add it to your chat bot setup. You will likely need to customize the output based on your needs, but I think this should be a pretty good basis for everyone to work from.

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
from chatterbot.adapters.logic.base_match import BaseMatchAdapter
from fuzzywuzzy import fuzz


class TheIDontKnowAdapter(BaseMatchAdapter):
    """
    This adapter checks if a closely matching response is known.
    """

    def get(self, input_statement):
        """
        Takes a statement string and a list of statement strings.
        Returns the closest matching statement from the list.
        """
        statement_list = self.context.storage.get_response_statements()

        default_response = Statement("I don't understand.")

        # Return the default response if there is no data in the database
        if not statement_list:
                return 1, default_response

        confidence = -1
        closest_match = input_statement

        # Find the closest matching known statement
        for statement in statement_list:
            ratio = fuzz.ratio(input_statement.text, statement.text)

            if ratio > confidence:
                confidence = ratio
                closest_match = statement

        # Return the default response if a close match cannot be found
        if confidence < 10:
            return 1, default_response

        return 0, closest_match
```

Not yet, but I'm hoping to add one eventually.

* should we consider "invite"/"accept" as the verb pair? something which speaks to the power being granted rather than the (relatively less important) direction of data transfer?
* I moved `--verify` and `-0` to be subcommand arguments already (see `CommonArgs` in src/wormhole/cli/cli.py), along with `-c=/--code-length=`, `--tor`, `--hide-progress` (although that's file-transfer -specific), and `--no-listen` (which is both file-transfer -specific and is a debug argument that I'd like to hide from the --help output).
* I kinda like the "YOLO" brevity implied by `-0`, but I guess it couldn't hurt to add a long-form name that's more.. discouraging, so if someone is demonstrating it in a slide deck or something, they can point out what it really does. Maybe `-0/--unauthenticated`? Or `-0/--yolo`? The help text must explain what is really does, of course.

Re `--allow-root-access` and `--user=` .. I guess the question is: what is the majority use case going to be? If we think that "root adds a new account" is the majority, then a mandatory `user=` positional argument seems promising, so the recommended setup recipe can be:

```
# adduser --disabled-password warner
# wormhole ssh invite warner
 -> CODE
```

And if you're logging into your own account and want to add a new key (maybe you used a password the first time, or maybe you logged in from one host and want to add the pubkey of a second host), you do the same:

```
% wormhole ssh invite warner
 -> CODE
```

If we think the add-key-to-my-own-account is more likely, then we'd not require the posarg, default to modifying your own `.ssh/authorized_keys`, add a way to point at the file or the user, and add extra protection against root modifying root's keys.

Re pubkeys: yeah, that plan sounds fine. The only thing I'd add would be to print out the list of discovered pubkeys, if there's more than one. And I think I'd be ok with leaving out a special check to avoid sending root's pubkey (doesn't seem dangerous to me). I might call it `-p=/--pub-key=` instead of authkey, although I could be swayed otherwise ("pub" matches the source file, "auth" matches the dest file, "key" matches the "ssh-keygen" tool that creates them).

For extra credit we could make it interactive (print a list, assign a number to each, ask the user for the number), but maybe that's too much work.

Unlike text/file sending, the sender doesn't need to confirm the transfer. But the receiver *does* (at least if `--verify` is enabled), since it's the receiver who's granting authority by accepting the transfer. Not sure how that might affect the common send/receive utilities.. there might need to be a mode flag that says which direction the verification needs to travel.

Well for the `evolve()`-like code, it gives y=10 because that's the default value set in `__init__`. Here's a more complete example:
```
      @dataclass
      class C:
          x: int
          y: int = field(init=False, default=10)
          z: int

      c = C(1, 4)  # those are x and z values: no value for y, since it has init=False
      c.y = 20
      # now, c == C(x=1, y=20, z=4)

      c1 = replace(c, x=5)
      # Creates a new instance by calling C(x=5, z=c.z)
      # So c1 == C(x=5, y=10, z=4)
```
The y=10 for c1 comes from it being set in `__init__()`, since that's the default value.

I'm not saying that's the preferred behavior, I'm saying that's how `attrs` works.

In my earlier message I said I prefer the `evolve()`-like behavior, but that was a typo (that I'm not going to edit, since it might change the responses).

I think the preferred approach to ` c1 = replace(c, x=5)` would give c1 as `C(x=5, y=20, z=4)`. That is, the equivalent of:
```
c1 = <new C object, not created by calling __init__>
c1.x = 5  # the new value given in the replace() call
c1.y = c.y  # for fields not given in replace(), use the value from the source object
c1.z = c.z
```
Even if it's slower, this is the path of least surprise.

optimizer.run(n_iters=1e3, objective_func=f, ineq_constraints=(g_1, g_2), eq_constraints=(h_1, h_2))
```

You could allow only inequality constraints, and require h(x) = 0 to be written as h(x) <= 0, -h(x) <= 0, but this seems unnecessarily complicated for the user.

What other options are there? You could define a class that holds the objective function and constraints, but that wouldn't be consistent with how you defined the other problem base classes. You could have a single vector-valued function that evaluates all of the constraints? I don't see a lot of upside to this.

Thoughts? Other options?

What the test does:

- create a file by passing `u'\xe9.txt'` to Python's `open()` (which ought to be translated into a native Win32 Unicode API call, AFAIU)
- add it into version control (using `['bzr', 'add', '--', u'\xe9.txt'.encode(locale.getpreferredencoding() ]` passed to `subprocess.Popen()`)
- call `subprocess.Popen(['bzr', 'ls', '-VR'])`, then decode the standard output using `sys.stdout.encoding` (or `sys.stdin.encoding`, or `locale.getpreferredencoding()`).
- expect to get `u'\xe9.txt'` back.

In my manual experiments on the Jenkins VM, `locale.getpreferredencoding()` returns cp1252, and `sys.stdout.encoding` is cp437.

U+00E9 is é.  In cp1252 it is represented by `\xe9`.  In cp437 it is represented by `\x82`.

When you decode `\xe9` using cp437, you get `\u0398`.

When you decode `\x82` using cp1252, you get `\u201a`.  Bingo!

Somehow, under Jenkins, the test is unable to determine the encoding from `sys.stdout`/`sys.stdin`, and therefore falls back to `locale.getpreferredencoding()`.

This is a HTTP POST request when adding data from the Django Admin
and  contains a character string that can not be decoded with latin1.

I think that only 'HTTP Header' needs 'WSGI encoding dance'.
Because Werkeug's 'WSGI encoding dance' is a conversion of Native String.

In PEP 3333, 'string' is treated as follows.

> WSGI therefore defines two kinds of "string":
> 
> "Native" strings (which are always implemented using the type named str) that are used for request/response headers and metadata
> "Bytestrings" (which are implemented using the bytes type in Python 3, and str elsewhere), that are used for the bodies of requests and responses (e.g. POST/PUT input data and HTML page outputs).
> Do not be confused however: even if Python's str type is actually Unicode "under the hood", the content of native strings must still be translatable to bytes via the Latin-1 encoding! (See the section on Unicode Issues later in this document for more details.)

https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3333/#a-note-on-string-types

There is Zappa in a similar library, but looking at the corresponding part of Zappa, it looks like this.

https://github.com/Miserlou/Zappa/blob/master/zappa/wsgi.py#L69
```
        if binary_support and (method in BINARY_METHODS):
            if event_info.get('isBase64Encoded', False):
                encoded_body = event_info['body']
                body = base64.b64decode(encoded_body)
            else:
                body = event_info['body']
                if isinstance(body, six.string_types):
                    body = body.encode("utf-8")

        else:
            body = event_info['body']
            if isinstance(body, six.string_types):
                body = body.encode("utf-8")
```

https://github.com/Miserlou/Zappa/blob/master/zappa/wsgi.py#L128
```
        environ['wsgi.input'] = six.BytesIO(body)
```

So, I thought 'WSGI encoding dance' to the Request body is unnecessary.

Hi @seanbayou.

I'm glad your daughter liked the animations.  It was to help inspire younger programmers that I originally wrote this code!  The samples (and associated built in clases) are there to show you what is possible building up layers on top of a modest set of features provided by the Screen class.

When it comes down to it, all animations really just draw the image, wait then move it a little (maybe based on some input) and finally clear up any bits that were left behind.  All the basic drawing and input required for this is covered in the Screen class.  I would recommend that you start by reading the docs for this.  They are here: http://asciimatics.readthedocs.org/en/stable/io.html

Once you've got the basics of drawing to a given location on the Screen, the simplest thing to do is probably to animate a ball (which you could represent with the letter 'O'). To start, let's just move it along a line.  The program would be something like a call to Screen.wrapper() to get your Screen object and call your main loop.  This main loop would then:

1. Draw the ball at its current location - say (0, 0) to start.
2. Sleep for a short time - say 0.1s.
3. Clear the current location - by drawing a space character there instead.
4. Update the current location - e.g. add 1 to the x coordinate.
5. Return to the start of the loop until you hit the edge of the Screen.

Once you've got that working, you could enhance it by making it bounce back to the start by reversing the movement operation (step 4).

	def moving_average(x, n, type='simple'):
		"""
		compute an n period moving average.

		type is 'simple' | 'exponential'

		"""
		x = np.asarray(x)
		if type == 'simple':
			weights = np.ones(n)
		else:
			weights = np.exp(np.linspace(-1., 0., n))

		weights /= weights.sum()

		a = np.convolve(x, weights, mode='full')[:len(x)]
		a[:n] = a[n]
		return a


	def relative_strength(prices, n=14):
		"""
		compute the n period relative strength indicator
		http://stockcharts.com/school/doku.php?id=chart_school:glossary_r#relativestrengthindex
		http://www.investopedia.com/terms/r/rsi.asp
		"""

		deltas = np.diff(prices)
		seed = deltas[:n+1]
		up = seed[seed >= 0].sum()/n
		down = -seed[seed < 0].sum()/n
		rs = up/down
		rsi = np.zeros_like(prices)
		rsi[:n] = 100. - 100./(1. + rs)

		for i in range(n, len(prices)):
			delta = deltas[i - 1]  # cause the diff is 1 shorter

			if delta > 0:
				upval = delta
				downval = 0.
			else:
				upval = 0.
				downval = -delta

			up = (up*(n - 1) + upval)/n
			down = (down*(n - 1) + downval)/n

			rs = up/down
			rsi[i] = 100. - 100./(1. + rs)

		return rsi


	def moving_average_convergence(x, nslow=26, nfast=12):
		"""
		compute the MACD (Moving Average Convergence/Divergence) using a fast and slow exponential moving avg'
		return value is emaslow, emafast, macd which are len(x) arrays
		"""
		emaslow = moving_average(x, nslow, type='exponential')
		emafast = moving_average(x, nfast, type='exponential')
		return emaslow, emafast, emafast - emaslow

Regarding the issue, it has nothing to do with `win-unicode-console`. Essentially same behaviour occurs without `win-unicode-console` as well. The point is that calling `exitˋ closes `sys.stdin`, so you get an exception whenever you try to read input from a user afterwards (e.g. in `atexit` handler). I don't know why `exit` closes `sys.stdin`, but there seems to be a reason: https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/default/Lib/_sitebuiltins.py#l23. However, if you use `sys.exit` instead of `exit`, it works fine.

First would be to use `@jwt_optional` if both the public and private data sets are used in parallel, and then distinguish in the endpoint itself which dataset to return to the caller based on if an access token is present or not. # this is untested

Doing some digging I found that the messages get passed down to socketio and eventually engineio, and in engineio they are queued for sending, which is handled by the `engineio.async_gevent_uwsgi.wait` method which will call `engineio.async_gevent_uwsgi._send` when it finds messages in its queue. The `_send` method eventually uses `uwsgi.websocket_send` to do the actual TX.

https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-engineio/blob/master/engineio/async_gevent_uwsgi.py#L107

The problem is that the first 3 messages (initial ping with SID, then always "40" followed by "40/<namespace>") are sent (see figure; white is TX, green is RX), then `engineio.async_gevent_uwsgi.wait` stops looping so despite more messages making their way into the queue, nothing gets sent because `wait` isn't checking the queue.

[WS](http://i.imgur.com/LrCqlPU.png)

If `follow_file()` returns/dies then `wait` resumes looping and sends messages.

Hi @EmiM - I was just investigating this very thing this week and I ended up with the following solution.

DjangoListObjectField actually does filtering and pagination, and returns totalCount. Ironically, DjangoFilterPaginateListField does not. Not sure why.

```
class ResourceType(DjangoObjectType):
    class Meta:
        description = "Type definition for resource"
        model = Resource
        filterset_class = ResourceFilter

class ResourceListType(DjangoListObjectType):
    class Meta:
        description = "Type definition for resources uses in list queries"
        model = Resource

class Query(object):
    resource = DjangoObjectField(ResourceType)
    resources = DjangoListObjectField(ResourceListType, filterset_class=ResourceFilter, description=('All Resources query'))
```

I hope this helps!

I believe there is still an issue when using source maps and a minified output. 

```
sass --style=compressed --sourcemaps=file style.scss compiled.css
```

This creates a single line output. However ``convert_urls`` will rewrite this file making the generated source map inaccurate. It would be better to first convert the urls and then apply the compiler. This however requires the creation of a temporary file, which then gets altered and then compiled. Nasty, but maybe the only way to do it.

A solution could look like this:

```python
    def convert_urls_to_tempfile(full_source_path, source_path):
        '''read the content of source_path, appy transformation to it, save it as a temporary and return path to that file'''
        _, tmp_path = tempfile.mkstemp(dir=os.path.dirname(full_source_path))
        with open(tmp_path, 'wb') as tmp_file:
            with open(full_source_path, 'rb') as source_file:
            tmp_file.write(
                url_converter.convert(source_file.read(),
                    source_path)

        return tmp_path

    
    def compile_file(self, source_path):
        full_source_path = self.get_full_source_path(source_path)
        full_output_path = self.get_full_output_path(source_path)
        full_output_dirname = os.path.dirname(full_output_path)

        content_path = copy_content_to_tempfile(full_source_path, source_path)

        try:
            args = [
                self.executable,
                "--sourcemap=none",
            ]

            if self.is_compass_enabled:
                args.append("--compass")

            args.extend([
                content_path,
                full_output_path,
            ])

            if not os.path.exists(full_output_dirname):
                os.makedirs(full_output_dirname)

            # `cwd` is a directory containing `source_path`.
            # Ex: source_path = '1/2/3', full_source_path = '/abc/1/2/3' -> cwd = '/abc'
            cwd = os.path.normpath(os.path.join(full_source_path, *([".."] * len(source_path.split("/")))))
            out, errors = utils.run_command(args, None, cwd=cwd)

            if errors:
                if os.path.exists(full_output_path):
                    os.remove(full_output_path)
                raise exceptions.StaticCompilationError(errors)

        finally:
            os.remove(content_path)

        return self.get_output_path(source_path)
```

Adding an additional constructor is our first consideration. But after observing the original interface: 
```scala
class NDArrayIter (data: IndexedSeq[NDArray], label: IndexedSeq[NDArray] = IndexedSeq.empty,
                  private val dataBatchSize: Int = 1, shuffle: Boolean = false,
                  lastBatchHandle: String = "pad",
                  dataName: String = "data", labelName: String = "label") extends DataIter {...
```
we found there is no position to place the custom names. Therefore, since each overloading constructor has to call primary constructor, keeping the original interface may not a possible way if we want to make using multiple input flexible.

Another reason we modifying the existing constructor is that we observer the fields defined in the class:
```scala
val initData: IndexedSeq[(String, NDArray)] = IO.initData(_dataList, false, dataName)
val initLabel: IndexedSeq[(String, NDArray)] = IO.initData(_labelList, true, labelName)
```
We guess maybe using the type `IndexedSeq[(String, NDArray)] ` is the original motivation at the beginning.

In Python there is a [way](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/io.py#L490) to manipulate multiple input. But Scala is static type. Maybe we can try to rethink about the API interface design.

Based on my experience of using fork() over many years, I would say that calling fork() from a Multi-Threaded process is typically NOT recommended. If you do, you will need to understand the details in how it works to make correct use of it. A few caveats on the use of fork() are:

1. The behavior of fork() can vary across operating systems. For example, Solaris has fork-one and fork-all model, whereas Linux has fork-one model. The fork-one model duplicates only the calling thread whereas the fork-all model duplicates all the calling threads.

2. The fork-one model can leave mutexes in bad state in the called thread. See excellent description of the problems in the classic book on Linux Programming Interface [2].

3. A good summary of the problem is here: [3]

4. More problems are described here: [4]. For example, you need to ensure that Close-on-exec flag is set for file-descriptors [depends on whether the programming language does it for you, C does not], otherwise two processes will have reference to the same file and close() from one process will NOT actually close the file since the file is also referenced from the other process. 

5. Long discussion on why fork without exec is dangerous in large programs [5]. I always used exec() after a fork() and set the Close-on-exec() for file-descriptions when I have called fork() from a MT process.

References:
[1] fork-one and fork-all model: https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19683-01/806-6867/gen-1/index.html
[2] The Linux Programming Interface: A Linux and UNIX System Programming Handbook
By Michael Kerrisk: https://books.google.com/books?id=Ps2SH727eCIC&pg=PA686&lpg=PA686&dq=fork+but+no+exec+mutex&source=bl&ots=kMBgx2BVwb&sig=PI74pt3fvO8gHKLLUaIUrr_shy8&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjTu8qn75_WAhVJ7GMKHb46BQIQ6AEIOjAH#v=onepage&q=fork%20but%20no%20exec%20mutex&f=false
[3] https://thorstenball.com/blog/2014/10/13/why-threads-cant-fork/
[4] http://www.linuxprogrammingblog.com/threads-and-fork-think-twice-before-using-them
[5] https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=12302539

A few points to consider:
* uris: it'd be great to have a URI scheme for dag locations as in `s3://mybucket/mydag.tar.gz` or `hdfs://dags_folders/mydag.1032910.zip`, or `local:///val/dags/mydag.py` there has to be a python lib that already works for fetching files from all sorts of places. The git one should somehow have support for a git ref in there like a some SHA `git://repolocation/{SOME_GIT_REF_WHICH_COULD_BE_MASTER_OR_SOME_SHA}/myfolder/mydag.py`
* serialization: there's support for `.zip` files now somehow, and I can see how it would be nice to have this extensible. Proper modeling would call for yet another abstraction for that, though my thought is that the base dag fetcher class can handle it in a method. We'll need some support for fetch, decompress in temp, keep-as-cached, cleanup flow.
* versioning: there has to be versioning semantics in the API here
* dags manifest: currently the DAGs list is inferred by crawling through the DAGS_FOLDER (yuck! my bad...), we need alternate ways of defining that. Providing a list of URIs would be a reasonable alternative. Perhaps there's a way to generate that list of URIs given a DAGS_FOLDER. `airflow_settings` could have a `DAG_LIST` configuration that defaults to the current DAG_LIST = `lambda x: DagBag.crawl_folder_for_dags(conf.get('DAGS_FOLDER'))`. has to have support for a callable as it may change over time, the scheduler should know when to re-evaluate the callable periodically. We'd probably expect people to move towards maintaining a list manually.
* assuming version semantics in URIs, perhaps pinning the URI in the DagRun instance. This would allow for version consistency within a DagRun

I think the URI is really key here. Assuming that the URI contains a provenance (s3, hive, hdfs, git), and can contain a version number (people are free to version their artifacts, create symlinks to latests, git refs, ...) and that an extension `.zip`, `.tar.gz` can call for some unpacking handling.

A few more thing:
* caching: hopefully we don't fetch-unpack-interpret when we don't need to, has to be deterministic somehow for different processes on the same box to reuse it. Not sure if caching logic should live (DagBag? DagFetcher?)
* would be great to move towards a more stateless web server, I'm not sure where that fits in, it's probably not a great idea to handle the DAG fetching within the web request's scope

Sorry for the large brain dump here. This is a bit of an hairy issue.

That last line of output: `Preserving output in /tmp/watchmantestF2v_AA` shows you where the logs from the run were stored.  What I typically do to run down the problems there is:

```
less /tmp/watchmantest*/inst*/log
```

Then press `G` to jump to the bottom and look for a stack trace; if I see one, then I go dig into the relevant portion of the code.  Otherwise, `:n` to go to the log file from the next instance and repeat.

You can also do:

```
rm -rf /tmp/watchmantest* ; ./runtests.py --concurrency=1 --keep-if-fail
```

to (clean up any test data from a prior run and) reduce the number of concurrent watchman instances run by the tests; that can be helpful in some situations.

If for some reason the backtrace isn't useful (depends on how good the `backtrace()` implementation is on Solaris), you can run this instead:

```
rm -rf /tmp/watchmantest* ; ./runtests.py --debug-watchman --keep-if-fail
```

and the test runner will print out the pid of the watchman instance and wait for you to attach to it with your favourite debugger before starting the tests so that you can use it to examine the stack trace.

I think this is intended behavior, albeit unexpected.   When the CLI decides that it needs to spawn the service on macOS it uses launchd, and we only allow a single instance per user, which is why the plist file is getting tromped.

If you were to run those instances using `--foreground` then we wouldn't try to use launchd and you could have them happily coexist.  We use that approach in the watchman test suite.

Why do you need to run multiple instances?  This shouldn't be necessary outside of integration testing watchman itself; one of the value props for using watchman is that it can aggregate watch resources in a single process to minimize the amount of duplicated work to watch various dirs.  Is there a use case that we're missing?

In fact, this logic is making me reconsider the argparse handling of lists and dicts that's currently in master. It's quite verbose and the implementation is rather complicated. With `.from_string`, we could enable things like:

    command --list-trait a,b,c

and parse that out, which seems a lot nicer than:

    command --list-trait a --list-trait b --list-trait c

and would be vastly simpler to implement. A disadvantage of the current mechanism is that, like the aborted env handling, it behaves differently for traits registered in the application.classes list vs classes not in that list (e.g. extensions), which is undesirable. If we leaned more heavily on from_string, then the difference would be eliminated, which seems desirable.

My point of view is that if any upstream task is failed or disabled, your
task will not run. It's a bit weird to not mark it reddish until it
completely stops getting new dependencies run. Seeing an accurate upstream
failed count makes it easier to know that there's a serious problem. Seeing
a job you're tracking marked as upstream failed tells you that there's
something you need to look into to make sure it gets done. Maybe you need
to fix it, maybe you need to ping another team to make sure they're on it.

I'll put something together to handle the test case. I didn't see that was
there!

On Thu, Nov 10, 2016 at 6:57 PM, Arash Rouhani <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> Meanwhile, my visualizer now gives a misleading under-count of the number
> of upstream disabled and failed jobs. It seems quite weird that this count
> should grow as more jobs complete successfully. If an important job is
> being blocked by a failure, I don't want to have to wait until every other
> dependency is complete to learn about it.
>
> Am I missing something here?
>
> What you say is correct. The interpretation of "task X is an upstream
> disabled task" changed with this patch:
>
>    - Before it meant "task X has some dependency which is disabled* (but
>    no dependency is worse off)"
>    - Now it reads "Nothing on X or it's dependencies is progressing! All
>    non-complete dependencies of X are (at least) disabled*".
>
> The *star because it's actually disabled+upstream disabled.
> ------------------------------
>
> I think both numbers are pretty definitions are hard to grasp and many
> luigi users don't care to learn the difference. But if any, I actually do
> agree that the former is more intuitive. I'm not sure if it's better
> though. The old definition is very sensitive. Imagine a big enterprise and
> a leaf task from team Z has failed, now team A knows nothing about the
> details of team Z's tasks, but they transitively depend on it. But still
> their task is marked as reddish even though most dependencies are
> progressing. However, team Z is probably fully aware of the problem,
> they've got their error emails and can see that their task is "FAILED" in
> the scheduler.
>
> I'm kind of open to reverting this if you really think the old way to
> count is more useful. As long as the test case from #1790
> <https://github.com/spotify/luigi/pull/1790> still passes.
>
> The only thing I'm against is making the two ways of counting
> configurable, as it is too detailed for the typical luigi user to care
> about.

For any future people seeing this, the problem was indeed in the Python name resolution. We had a race condition introduced when forking a subprocess. An HTTP server thread that was normally only running in the main process was also being created in the subprocesses. This raced against boto/botocore first creating a connection to a service (S3, SQS) that needed a name resolution first. Moving the HTTP server start after the forking fixed the race condition and deadlock has not been encountered since.

See: http://bugs.python.org/issue25924
I will also continue to investigate our code here -- I have a feeling it's related to threading. We do try to use a separate session / client per-thread.

``result_keys`` method is to get the result_keys from the pagination model. So here is s3's pagination model: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/aws/s3/2006-03-01.paginators.json. It pretty much says what element in the response we are paginating for. So for ListObjects we are paginating for CommonPrefixes and Contents (which are the keys).

``result_keys_iter`` Allows use to return iterators for the values of the result_keys in the response. Note that since you can have more than one result_keys in the model, you can have more than one iterator returned by this method (one for each result_key).

@jamesls Good question!

If you're doing a cross-region copy, encrypted or otherwise, you send an API call to the destination region, and the `SourceDBSnapshotIdentifier` parameter must be an snapshot ARN, which encodes the source region in it already. In the unencrypted case, this is enough to proceed.

In the encrypted case, the request must also have a `PreSignedUrl` parameter, for KMS/crypto/authentication/security reasons. boto3 (very helpfully) synthesizes this complicated parameter for us, but to do that, it needs to know the source region, in order to use the `request_signer` to create a presigned URL valid in the source region. (See `_inject_presigned_url_rds()` calling `_get_cross_region_presigned_url` in `botocore/handlers.py`) In order to get the source region for this purpose, boto3 requires that you pass it in.

There's an alternative approach here, where boto3 can do some more introspection on `SourceDBSnapshotIdentifier` in `copy_db_snapshots`, and if the `SourceDBSnapshotIdentifier` is an ARN, it can attempt to parse the ARN, get the region from the ARN, see if the region from the ARN is different from the destination region (because you can do inside-region copies[1]), see if `KmsKeyId` is set (to tell whether the source snapshot is encrypted), and if all of that is true, use the region parsed from the source snapshot ARN to create the `PreSignedUrl`.

(It's worth noting from an implementation perspective that right now the lack of a `SourceRegion` parameter passed by the caller is used to avoid generating a pre-signed URL at all, so we want some of the heuristics above to keep current behavior where we're not creating a `PreSignedUrl` and passing it to an API all for no reason.)

There; I hope you don't regret asking. :-)

(This is decently complicated, so if you want a table outlining the parameters necessary for the Cartesian product of (unencrypted, encrypted) x (local-to-region, cross-region) copies, I can make that appear.)

[1] This check may not be necessary, but I'm not sure, and I haven't tried yet. The documentation says if you're doing an in-region copy to just provide a non-ARN DB snapshot identifier (e.g. `rds:mysql-instance1-snapshot-20130805`) rather than an ARN (e.g. `arn:aws:rds:us-west-2:123456789012:snapshot:mysql-instance1-snapshot-20130805`) but I suspect that if you passed in an ARN for a same-region copy, that it would silently just work...

The chosen [implementation](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/cmd.py#L48) uses `poll()` if possible, but resorts to brute-force threads for 'pumping' lines otherwise. The only reason threads are less desirable is that they have more synchronisation overhead in general, and are note reused.
Using a threadpool could help alleviate this overhead.

Please also note that to my surprise, `poll()` is not available on py2 on OSX either, but is available on py3 on OSX.
On windows, there is no `poll()` either, and the existing `select()` call only works on sockets. Therefore using threads was the only viable alternate code-path that would work everywhere.
Also note that I managed to have the thread implementation occasionally fail on me, which could hint at some sync issue still present.

I concur with @tomkcook's analysis that `Popen()` is throwing `PermissionError` because it can't read `cwd`, not because it can't find `git`. Thus, the error message shown above is inaccurate. To wit:

1. On initialization of the module, [the `refresh()` function is called with no arguments](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/2.1.8/git/__init__.py#L82), meaning `path` defaults to `None`.
1. That means that [the `Git.refresh()` classmethod is also called with `path` set to `None`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/2.1.8/git/__init__.py#L73).
1. Which, in turn, means that [the `Git` class is instantiated with `working_dir` set to `None`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/2.1.8/git/cmd.py#L525), so its `self._working_dir` attribute is `None` as well. 
1. So when [`Git.refresh()` attempts to invoke the "git version" command](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/2.1.8/git/cmd.py#L230) as a sanity test, it means [`execute()` has no value for `self._working_dir`, so it falls back to using `os.getcwd()`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/2.1.8/git/cmd.py#L689).
1. Thus, in the case where `cwd` is a path that the user cannot access, [the call to `Popen()`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/2.1.8/git/cmd.py#L721-L733) fails with the ambiguous `PermissionError`. 

Aside from the error message being inaccurate, I question whether GitPython actually needs to use the current working directory in this context. If there's a need to preserve defaulting to the current working directory, perhaps it's worth confirming that it can be accessed (e.g. with `os.access()`) before passing it to `Popen()`?

In GitPython, there is no magic behind `4b825dc642cb6eb9a060e54bf8d69288fbee4904`. `repo.index.diff` apparently attempts to convert the given SHA into an object using [`repo.rev_parse()`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/index/base.py#L1195), which must fail as there is no such object.

The SHA mentioned here is indeed magic, and used behind `EMPTY_TREE_SHA1_HEX` in the git source code - there is special handling that GitPython completely ignores, i.e. does not implement. Therefore it currently seems impossible to deal with the empty-repository case more gracefully than to catching exceptions and falling back to manually parsing the output of `repo.git.status()`.

The situation is further complicated by the transition from Py2-->PY3, I know.
But for the specific problem you describe, [surrogate escaping (PEP 383)](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0383/) seems like a perfect fit to this case.
So instead of using plain `utf-8`, just use `decode('utf-8', 'surrogateescape')`.

Adding `headless` argument for `Open Browser` is bit tricky. For naturally headless browsers it does not make much sense and for rest it felt weird doing since the implementation is not really there. Anyway, here's one suggestion https://github.com/Harri/SeleniumLibrary/commit/822285209b616879b336a2c31f7cc0066165cc69

I agree that it would be really nice to build the support for future headless browsers right away, it just feels weird. For example how should PhantomJS react to headless=True/False? What should be built for IE, which most probably will not ever get headless support? For me it feels cleanest to only support existing features. Also it is convenient to handle headless browsers as different browsers, since most tooling already allow specifying the browsers, not adding artificial arguments. Yes, it probably means extra work if and when Egde, Android and Safari get headless support.

Also, as I can see in Selenium2Library v.1.8.0 there was no handling of `ElementNotFound` or `WebDriverException`.  Keyword `wait_until_element_is_not_visible` is calling `_wait_until_no_error` raising `AssertionError` only on timeout (`_is_visible` inside was using `self._element_find(locator, True, False)` and returning `None` if no element is found). 
Maybe I've missed something though

Consider this simple scenario. There are packages A, B, C, and D.

* A depends on B and C.
* B depends on C and D.

And you specify A in your Pipfile.

The resolver can be thought of working in rounds. For each round, we look inside each package to find their dependencies, add them into the list, and repeat, until we cannot find anymore.

R0: A.
R1: A depends on B and C. Now we have A, B, C.
R2: B depends on C and D; C does not depend on anything. Now A, B, C, D.
R3: D does not depend on anything. Still A, B, C, D. Resolution finished successfully.

Now let’s consider versions. Say A, B, and D each has one version (to keeps things simple), while C has two, C1 and C2.

* A depends on B and C.
* B depends on C and D, *but requires C1* (i.e. specifies C<2 as a dependency).

R0: A.
R1: A wants on B and C. Which C should we choose? We don’t know.

What Pipenv current does is to always prefer the latest, so…

R1: A, B, C2.
R2: B wants C1 and D. Now we have a problem, we can’t install C1 and C2 at the same time. Resolution fails.

What backtracking allows, is to save before we made that decision in round 1.

R1: A, B, C… save here.
R1a: A, B, C2.
R2-1a: This does not work. Load.
R1b: A, B, C1.
R2-1b: A, B, C1, D. Success!

Now generalise this. Package X may require P<5 in v1, and P>5 in v2. We might choose v2 first, but if that prooves to be a problem later on (perhaps Y requires P<5 in all versions), we can backtrack and choose the v1 series of X instead. Those conflicting version requirements you see generally come from this kind of more complicated graphs, and backtracking is the solution to it (if there is a solution at all).

Backtracking resolution algorithm is largely a solved problem, and there are implementations in various languages. Python, however, is more lacking in this regard. [Mixology](https://pypi.org/project/mixology/), which is used by [Poetry](https://github.com/sdispater/poetry) (another tool that also uses a lock file), and largely similar to Ruby’s [Molinillo](https://github.com/CocoaPods/Molinillo) (used by Bundler and Cocoapods), is the only one I know in real-world production use. [Zazo](https://github.com/pradyunsg/zazo) is another, but it is in an even earlier stage.

I hope the above clarifies things somewhat. This is not a simple topic to work on (though again, not difficult, because everything is already solved, you only need to implement it with a straight head), but would help Pipenv—and I’dsay, the whole Python comminuty—tremendously, if done correctly.

My suggestion would be that someone (probably someone affected by this issue, but at a minimum someone on a platform that has the problem and also has a way of solving it) provide a pull request implementing a check along the lines of: 

* If we have spaces in the pathname,
* and we are on platform XXX,
* then write the shebang line with the following escaping to handle the spaces.
* In all other cases, fall back to the current behaviour.

Further additions could then be made by interested parties just adding extra platform checks.

Ideally, it would be nice to include a comment with a link to the documentation that confirms how platform XXX supports paths with spaces, so that future maintainers have a reference to check against. Personally, I'm not clear what fixes work and where:

1. the discussion here suggests that double quotes work on OSX, but does that depend on the precise OSX version?
2. In #611 escaping spaces with backslashes was used, but there was no confirmation as to what OS it was for (Linux? A specific kernel version? A specific distro?)

Note that no such platform-specific variants should use ```/usr/bin/env```. As @merwok pointed out, that results in a change in behaviour - the shebang is deliberately written to allow running the script *without* activating the environment.

Adding some tests to be sure the behaviour is as expected (including the principle that it falls back when we're not on a specifically recognised platform) would be extremely useful, too, but it would also be fiddly, as it would involve monkeypatching to allow testing for platform XXX when you're not actually running on that platform.

@VRGhost Is this really something that's desirable? 
Flask used to have something like this, but it turned out that keeping all extensions under a namespace package was just a pain for everyone involved, for no real benefit. It was quickly deprecated (first "soft"-deprecated by just telling people to not do that, and now properly). 

The import rewrite mechanism that rewrites `flask.ext.foo` to `flask_foo` was introduced BECAUSE of that, to make transition from .ext to `flask_foo` easier (backwards compat for apps using .ext).

Today, flask extensions are named `flask_foo` without any formalized checks, just by convention.

I think there's a slight mismatch between Werkzeug's model of the world here and Klein's.

The reason that I filed this bug, and the reason I expected this behavior, is that twisted.web's model of the world is that `/` is a Resource, and `/foo` is another Resource, and `/foo/bar` is another Resource, and so on.  Returning `None` from `getChild` returns `None` as a *Resource*, which is pretty clearly a 404: we tried to find something, instead we found `None`, i.e. we found nothing, i.e. 404.

The inconsistency here is that Klein can dispatch to a `Resource` object by returning said `Resource` object from a route.  However, the only way to consistently get the dispatch-to-an-arbitrary-`Resource`-from-an-arbitrary-route behavior is to make it a branch route.  However, I think I buy your analogy to `*args`, so... maybe this works as expected?

On the other hand, maybe this is a place where we should refuse the temptation to guess, and just say that a `None` coming back from a route is a 500, and you should always return some valid value.  The main reason that allowing a `None` return value is useful is that it's the default return value of a method, but it's not like there's a lot of utility in declaring routes just to return `None` from them frequently - if you want a `404` or a `204` or what have you, it's best to be explicit about that.

But since my mind has recently changed here I'm not too sure.  Thoughts?

@waypointsoftware are you using pykafka to produce messages to kafka, or some other client? If you're using pykafka on the producer side, there may be a simple answer to this, since the producer does have a default `linger_ms` of five seconds and a default `min_queued_messages` of 70000. This means that if you're only sending 150 messages per second, the pykafka producer will wait the full five seconds before flushing a single batched request to the broker.

If this sounds like your situation, you can probably fix it by adjusting `min_queued_messages` and/or `linger_ms` in the `Producer()` constructor - see the [`Producer.__init__` docs](http://pykafka.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/producer.html#pykafka.producer.Producer.__init__) for specifics.

I'm treating this issue as the reference for the DSL improvements of streamparse. This issue has become a focus in recent project team discussion. A few things to add:

#136 is related.

A perspective to keep in mind: analysts and "accidental" programmers benefit from a common syntax throughout their projects.

A reference doc:
https://storm.apache.org/documentation/Defining-a-non-jvm-language-dsl-for-storm.html

Correct.  The `storm shell` command referenced [here](https://storm.apache.org/documentation/Defining-a-non-jvm-language-dsl-for-storm.html) does three things to facilitate non-JVM DSLs:

1.  It packages all your code up in a JAR for you.
2.  Uploads the JAR to Nimbus
3.  Calls a script of your choosing with the nimbus host, port, and uploaded JAR location as command-line arguments.

It's the script that's called in the third step that actually needs to connect to Nimbus and submits the topology Thrift definition after the code is already in place on the server.

I feel like their is some context missing from this issue, because it seems unclear why we would want to split the conversion from the Python Topology DSL into the Thrift definition out into a separate tool.

It would have to be more than a topology-level thing, since it would have to run on every machine the topology is running on.  One way to avoid locals and race conditions would be to make each shell component run in its own independent environment.  

If we were using conda, we wouldn't even need to store multiple copies of everything that way, because conda hardlinks packages to each other (and has its own locking mechanism to make sure two conda commands aren't messing with the package index at the same time).

I had a similar issue with the error `channel 2: open failed: connect failed`, followed by some thrift open error. In my case, I discovered two causes. 
1. zookeeper/nimbus down. In this case, errors also shows up in storm UI;
2. wrong nimbus port in `config.json`. I typed `netstat -al` on the leader machine to find out that the port is not 6627 but 6629. So I changed the nimbus hosts to `XX.XX.XX.XX:6629` but kept the workers' port untouched, like `["XX.XX.XX.XX"]`, then everything worked.

@soupault: The following list is all the unique integer types in numpy

* `np.byte`
* `np.short`
* `np.intc`
* `np.int_`
* `np.longlong`
* `np.ubyte`
* `np.ushort`
* `np.uintc`
* `np.uint`
* `np.ulonglong`

Every other type is just an alias to one of these (ie, `np.t1 is np.t2`).

Unfortunately to make it more confusing, some of these types have the same `__name__` - but this shouldn't matter in your implementation.

The definition for these names is sadly all over the place - you can see a glimpse of the issue in numpy/numpy#10151

> I would rather just see "max_batch_endpoint", dropping the "cliquet" prefix.

I put the prefix deliberately...

Pros:
- its name consistent with the setting name in ``.ini``
- it provides a namespace to some custom settings from application extending cliquet (e.g. ``kinto.flush_enabled``...)

Cons:
- it exposes internal notions (i.e. cliquet) to the client.
- unless cliquet is the name of the protocol, it imposes the word cliquet to other implementations of the server 
- (it is seemly long)

> I have a problem with the documentation of permissions.

Thanks for this feedback ! ...and your patience !

> Is the permission_backend set by default? How to set it on .ini file?

Nope, like storage, you need to at least define `cliquet.permission_backend` and `cliquet.permission_url` settings.

> How do you change permissions?

Permissions can be changed on records by those who have write access (ie. creator of record, or principals mentioned in `resource_name>_write_principals`)

> how to made all records public?

`champion_read_principals = system.Everyone` should work (careful about singular in resource name)

However (*and the documentation might be really bad about this*), you have to use a `ProtectedResource` and a `RouteFactory`

You can find an example here :  https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/blob/118d1235a309f19616386f36c3ca2c58ef491c07/cliquet/tests/testapp/__init__.py#L15

> Is it possible to hide a field of a record according to permissions?

Yes, with some hacks... overriding the resource class:

(*untested*)

```python
class HiddenFields(ProtectedResource):
    def postprocess(self, *args, **kwargs):
        result = super(HiddenFields, self).postprocess(*args, **kwargs)
        if self.request.prefix_authenticated_id:
            return result
        if isinstance(result['data'], list):
            # collection endpoint.
            for record in result['data']:
                record.pop('secret', None)
        else:
            result['data'].pop('secret', None)
        return result

    def _raise_412_if_modified(self, record=None):
        if record and not self.request.prefix_authenticated_id:
            record.pop('secret', None)
        return super(HiddenFields, self)._raise_412_if_modified(record)
```

This implementation is wrong. There should be two separate tokens. There should be a refresh token used exclusively with the identity provider to get new access tokens with a long expiration period (max session length). There should be an access token that is used exclusively with relying parties, that has a short expiration period. When an access token expires the refresh token is used to get a new access token. 

JWTs and far from perfect and this is a poor/dangerous implementation that should be avoided.

It should be simple to write a function that shifts data called `lag` or whatever you want, and use it with syntax like

```
varalpha ~ 1 + lag(varalpha,1)
```

since `patsy` will default drop rows with missing observations.  The function `lag` would look something like

```
import pandas as pd

def lag(x, n):
    if n == 0:
        return x
    if isinstance(x,pd.Series):
        return x.shift(n)

    x = x.copy()
    x[n:] = x[0:-n]
    x[:n] = np.nan
    return x
```

and the use

```
y  = np.random.randn(100)
df = pd.DataFrame({'y':y})
lhs, rhs = dmatrices('y ~ 1 + lag(y,1)',df,return_type='dataframe')

print(np.all(lhs.iloc[:-1,0].values == rhs.iloc[1:,1].values))

True
```

Sorry for the confusions.

--input_sentence_size: The number of lines spm_train first loads. Remaining lines are simply discarded.  Since spm_train loads entire corpus into memory, this size will depend on the memory size of the machine. It also affects training time.

--training_sentence_size: The number of lines to train BPE/unigram model.

--mining_sentence_size: The number of lines to generate seed pieces. spm_train extracts frequent substring from the corpus with suffix array, which requires O(10N) memory space.  This setting is valid when --model_type=unigram.  

--seed_sentencepiece_size:  The size of seed pieces. This setting is valid when --model_type=unigram.

In general, we can use the default values except for --input_sentence_size.  Training spm with more than 10M sentences requires tons of memory and CPU resources.

@twiecki I figured by cutting the factors into quantiles, the interaction plot would be simliar to the returns tear sheet where it displays mean return by quantile. It would also allow the user to look at the interaction of classifiers with other classifiers (as opposed to just factors).

That said, I agree with you. We should probably use a regression method as long as a continuous factor is provided for the x-axis. (However, the 2nd factor would still need to be cut into quantiles)

I also like the idea of providing an option as to the regression/smoothing method used (e.g. OLS or LOWESS). Something like LOWESS would allow the user to analyze non-linear relationships. Here is an example of what the plot might look like using some simulated factor and returns data.
![smoothers](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28760724/32743330-13c956fa-c87a-11e7-9fc5-2f03c0dced8c.png)

Here is the code used to generate these plots along with some additional output for context:

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib as mpl
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Set number of simulated data points
num_obs = 1000

np.random.seed(88)

# Create two uncorrelated factors (starndardized to mu=0, sigma=1)
factor_1 = np.random.normal(size=num_obs)
factor_2 = np.random.normal(size=num_obs)

# Cut Factor_2 into quantiles
q2 = pd.qcut(factor_2, 3, labels=[1,2,3])

# Create Simulated Returns
returns = (np.exp(0.8*factor_1) + 
           0.3*factor_2 + 
           0.5*factor_1*factor_2 + 
           np.random.normal(0,1,num_obs))

# Combine all of the above into a DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({'factor_1': factor_1, 
                   'factor_2': factor_2, 
                   'returns': returns, 
                   'quantile_2':q2})
print df.head()
```

```python
def plot_scatter_grid(df):
    """Plot a grid of scatter plots faceted by Factor 2 Quantile """
    plt.figure(figsize=(10,10))
    for q in range(3):
        # Get Data for the Quantile q+1
        mask = df['quantile_2']==(q+1)
        data = df.loc[mask,[ 'factor_1', 'returns']]
        
        # Create Scatter Plot
        color = cmap(q)
        plt.subplot(2,2,q+1)
        data.plot(x='factor_1', y='returns', 
                  kind='scatter', ax=plt.gca() , 
                  color = color, alpha=0.5,
                  title=("Q%s of Factor 2" % (q+1)))
        
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
       
plot_scatter_grid(df)
